title: Writing - Part 2
description: Choose one of the following writing tasks and write your answer in 140-190 words.

tasks.title: Article
tasks.prompt:
You see this notice in an English-language magazine:

'Music is everywhere — but do we really listen to it anymore? Write an article about the role music plays in your life and whether you think live music is better than recorded music.'

Write your article.
tasks.modelAnswer:
The Sound of Life: Why Music Still Matters

Put in your earphones, press play, and within seconds your mood can completely transform. Music has an almost magical ability to make us feel happy, nostalgic, energised, or calm. For me, it has always been one of the most important parts of daily life.

I listen to music while commuting, exercising, and studying. Different genres serve different purposes — upbeat pop for workouts, classical pieces for concentration, and acoustic folk when I need to unwind. Music is not just background noise; it is a companion.

But nothing compares to seeing live music. The energy of a concert, the connection between performers and audience, and the imperfections that make each performance unique — these are things that a recording can never fully capture. I have been to dozens of concerts, and every single one has left me feeling genuinely alive.

In a world increasingly dominated by algorithms and playlists, I encourage everyone to experience music live at least once. It truly is something else.

tasks.title: Email
tasks.prompt:
Your English friend Sam has invited you to a music festival next summer and suggested you both go together. Write an email to Sam saying whether you can go, asking for details about the festival, and suggesting what you could do to prepare for the trip.

Write your email to Sam.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Hi Sam,

Thank you so much for the invitation — going to a music festival together sounds absolutely amazing! I would love to come, and I'm already really excited about it.

Could you send me some more details about the festival? I'd love to know which artists are performing, how many days it lasts, and roughly how much the tickets cost. Also, would we camp on site or stay in nearby accommodation? I've never camped at a festival before, so I'm a little nervous about that!

As for preparation, I think we should start planning well in advance. We could make a shared list of essentials to pack — things like waterproof clothing, portable phone chargers, and sunscreen. I'd also suggest we research the line-up together and mark the acts we definitely don't want to miss so we can plan our days efficiently.

I can't wait! Let me know when you have all the details.

Speak soon,
Rio

tasks.title: Letter
tasks.prompt:
You read a letter in a local newspaper arguing that music lessons should be removed from school timetables to make room for more academic subjects. Write a letter to the editor disagreeing with this view and explaining why music education is important.

Write your letter.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Dear Editor,

I am writing in response to the letter published on Tuesday suggesting that music lessons should be cut from the school curriculum. I strongly disagree with this proposal and feel compelled to share my perspective.

Music education offers benefits that go far beyond learning to play an instrument. Research consistently shows that studying music improves mathematical ability, enhances memory, and develops discipline and perseverance. These are precisely the qualities that schools are trying to foster through academic subjects.

Furthermore, for many students, music is the subject in which they truly excel and find their passion. Removing it would not only reduce enjoyment of school but could also discourage creative and artistic young people from engaging with education altogether.

The arts are not a luxury add-on to a curriculum — they are an essential part of a balanced and enriching education. Countries that invest in arts education consistently produce well-rounded, innovative graduates.

I urge the school authorities to protect music lessons rather than sacrifice them.

Yours faithfully,
Eleanor Baxter
